original_abstract: This paper examines the load-bearing capacity of steel railway bridges constructed during the second half of the 19th century, serving as a critical discussion of findings recently published in the journal "Roads and Bridges - Drogi i Mosty" (No. 2, 2014). The analysis evaluates the structural integrity of these historical assets in light of modern Eurocode standards, specifically addressing a bridge dismantled in 2007 after 122 years of service. A central point of contention in current engineering assessments is the appropriate yield strength value to assume for aged steel; while laboratory tests often yield higher averages, standardized safety protocols may necessitate more conservative estimates. It is concluded that any accurate verification of the load-carrying capacity of long-exploited bridges must go beyond initial delivery parameters and account for the long-term effects of spontaneous aging on material properties. Furthermore, the discussion emphasizes that while modern safety criteria are rigorous, their application to historical structures requires a balanced approach to avoid unnecessary demolitions of viable infrastructure.
humanized_abstract: The paper is an extension of the discussion concerning the strength capacity of 19th century steel railway bridges, which was originally published in the journal “Roads and Bridges – Drogi i Mosty” (No. 2, 2014). Within the scope of the discussion, the authors investigate the strength capacity of an iron bridge that was demolished in 2007, after 122 years of operation. The dispute in the current strength assessment of operational structures concerns the appropriate value of the yield strength of the steel to be adopted. The results of laboratory tests on a steel sample often indicate higher average values than the design values adopted in the strength calculation by taking into account the factor of safety of the standard. The assessment of the strength capacity of long operated structures cannot be performed solely on the strength values of the structure as it was delivered. The structure, together with the climate, has “evolved” over the years and the spontaneous aging processes have been expressed in the actual strength capacity of the structure materials. The existing design criteria for strength and safety control in modern structural engineering are very strict, but when it comes to old structures, their application should not be carried out strictly, in order to avoid demolition of operational and useful structures.
